behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches expected interface (parameter names, types, return type)
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is not in parsing logic but in whether the real LLM actually produces the correct signature given a natural language description. LLM-DEP: Correctness of the signature is entirely determined by real LLM output — a mocked LLM can be made to return any signature, so mocking hides the real failure. The test must call the real model and inspect actual generated output. INTEGRATION-CHECK does not apply because we are already above Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real code generation service with a well-specified function description (including typed parameters and return type). Extract the function signature from the generated source. Assert that parameter names, type annotations, and return type exactly match the specification. Run multiple times to check for non-deterministic regressions.

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Whether the code passes tests depends on real LLM output — mocking hides the real failure. MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly covers 'subsequent attempt', meaning generate → test → refine → test must all execute with real LLM calls. A single Agentic call only covers the first-attempt case; verifying that the system succeeds across attempts requires the full sequential agentic loop. → Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Using the real LLM, invoke the full pipeline on a function where the first LLM attempt frequently fails (e.g., a sorting function with edge-case requirements). Assert that the implementation returned — regardless of which attempt produced it — passes every test case in the provided suite. Confirm the pipeline terminates normally (not via the 3-failure error path).

behaviors.description: Refinement step includes failure messages from the previous attempt in the next LLM prompt
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The real failure mode is that the pipeline fails to route test failure messages from the test runner to the prompt builder — a cross-module wiring bug, not a bug inside a single function. Down-test: You could unit test a 'build_refinement_prompt' function in isolation, but that would miss the failure where the system never passes failure messages to that function. The module boundary between test runner output and prompt construction must be exercised. MOCK-HIDE: LLM can be mocked (return known broken code); the assertion is on the *prompt content*, not on LLM behavior. REAL-INFRA: In-process module wiring is sufficient. → Integration survives both checks.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the pipeline with a mock LLM whose first call returns known-broken code (e.g., missing a branch). Configure the test runner (real or stub) to produce specific, distinct failure messages. Capture the prompt sent to the LLM for the second (refinement) call. Assert the captured prompt contains the exact failure messages from the first test run, confirming the pipeline correctly threads failure context into the refinement step.

behaviors.description: Service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error containing all three attempts and their test results
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The stopping condition and structured error aggregation span the retry manager, test runner, and result collector — a cross-module interaction. Down-test: You cannot reproduce the '3-attempt stop + aggregate all results' failure by calling a single function with crafted args without wiring the loop. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the LLM to always fail and mocking the test runner to always report failures does not hide the real failure — the behavior under test is the retry count and structured error format, both deterministic once dependencies are injected. REAL-INFRA: In-process module wiring is sufficient; no real LLM or OS needed. → Integration survives both INTEGRATION-CHECK legs.
behaviors.test_description: Inject a mock LLM that always returns syntactically valid but logically wrong code and a stub test runner that always reports the same failure messages. Invoke the service. Assert: (1) exactly 3 LLM calls were made, (2) the service returns a structured error (not a working implementation), and (3) the error payload contains 3 attempt records, each carrying the generated code and the corresponding test failure output.

behaviors.description: Full generate → test → refine → test loop produces a working implementation for a function a single LLM call typically gets wrong
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior is explicitly defined as a sequential agentic pipeline: generate (LLM call 1) → test → refine (LLM call 2+) → test again. Each step depends on prior real LLM output and real test execution. LLM-DEP: The entire premise is that a *single* LLM call fails; the loop must produce correct output through iteration — both the failure and the correction require real model responses. No mock or stub can reproduce 'gets it wrong first but right later' without real non-determinism. → Workflow is the only level that can falsify this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Using the real LLM, run the full pipeline on a canonical 'hard-for-LLMs' function (e.g., one requiring correct handling of empty input, overflow, and off-by-one simultaneously) with a curated test suite that reliably exposes first-attempt errors. Assert that: (a) the first generated attempt fails at least one test, and (b) by the end of the loop the returned implementation passes all tests. This confirms the iterative refinement adds value over a single LLM call.
